g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Consensus and Related Problems in distributed systems.
Consensus is when multiple processes in a distributed system agree on a single value despite failures.
g.text: The fundamental problem where multiple independent nodes must agree on a single value
despite message delays, lost messages, and process failures.

g.data-narration: Consensus enables critical applications like blockchains, distributed databases, and cloud computing platforms.
g.text: Real-world applications that rely on consensus:

g.data-narration: A correct consensus protocol must satisfy three properties: Agreement, Validity, and Termination.
Distributed systems face three types of failures: crash, omission, and Byzantine - each requiring different handling.
g.text: Types of Failures in Distributed Systems

g.data-narration: The Byzantine Generals Problem illustrates consensus challenges with faulty components that may lie.
g.text: Classic problem introduced by Lamport, Shostak & Pease (1982)
All loyal generals must agree on same plan despite traitor(s)

g.data-narration: Byzantine consensus requires N > 3F, meaning 3F+1 nodes to tolerate F Byzantine failures.
g.text: N = total processes | F = faulty processes

g.data-narration: Paxos is a family of consensus algorithms by Leslie Lamport achieving consensus with crash fault tolerance.
g.text: Developed by Leslie Lamport (1989) - Most famous consensus algorithm
Achieves consensus in asynchronous networks with crash failures

g.data-narration: Paxos operates in three phases: Prepare, Accept, and Learn - using promises and majority voting.
g.text: Key: Acceptors promise not to accept proposals with number < N

g.data-narration: Raft is a more understandable consensus algorithm designed in 2014 by Ongaro and Ousterhout.
g.text: Designed by Diego Ongaro & John Ousterhout (2014) - More understandable than Paxos

g.data-narration: Raft uses randomized timeouts for leader election. Candidates request votes, majority wins leadership.
g.text: 1. Leader crashes → Followers wait for election timeout
2. Candidate increments term, votes for self, sends RequestVote

g.data-narration: In Raft, the leader appends log entries and replicates them to followers. Majority acknowledgment commits.
Let's review the key formulas: Byzantine needs N greater than 3F, and majority requires more than N/2 votes.
To summarize: Consensus requires agreement, validity, and termination. Paxos and Raft provide practical solutions.
